Details: Removal of 1,4-dioxane by biological filtration was examined in two bench-scale studies [2098, 2731] and one full-scale study [2733]. In the first bench study, approximately 93 to 97 percent contaminant removal was achieved with a microbial consortium enriched from a contaminated aquifer [2098]. The study was carried out in a continuous-flow attached-growth reactor in the presence of tetrahydrofuran at a high temperature (35 ºC) with long filter run times (384 to 600 hours), which are atypical conditions for drinking water treatment [2098]. In another bench-scale study [2731], approximately 94 to 96 percent contaminant removal was achieved by treating contaminated groundwater in two different types of reactors (JOB5 and CB1190) with granular activated carbon filter media. This study employed 120 hours of detention time, also atypical of drinking water treatment. A full-scale study using granular activated carbon media to support biological growth [2733] found only 28 percent contaminant removal in secondary effluent from a reclamation plant. Thus, biological filtration may not be suitable for 1,4-dioxane in full-scale water treatment applications.
